toc.value: 1) Find the NVMe partition and UUID
Optional: make the mount writable for your user

frontMatter.title: How to Auto-Mount an NVMe Drive on Linux at Startup (Using /etc/fstab)
frontMatter.author: Amrut Prabhu
frontMatter.summary: Auto-mount your NVMe SSD on Linux at startup using /etc/fstab the right way. Learn how to find the UUID, create a mount point, add a safe fstab entry

suggestedArticles.title: How to Tune CPU FAN Speed With PWM on Armbian
Radxa Cubie A5E Hands-On: The Budget NVMe SBC
How to Back Up an SD Card (Full OS Image) to an IMG File Using Linux

prev.title: How to Back Up an SD Card (Full OS Image) to an IMG File Using Linux Command Line
prev.author: Amrut Prabhu
prev.summary: Create a complete SD card backup image (.img or .img.gz) on Linux using command line tools. Restore the image anytime to recover an OS and all installed software if the card gets corrupted.

suggestedArticles.title: Radxa Cubie A7A Review: Pi-Sized Power With PCIe, NVMe, USB 3.1 Gen2
How to Tune CPU FAN Speed With PWM on Armbian
How to Auto-Mount an NVMe Drive on Linux at Startup

next.title: How to Tune CPU FAN Speed With PWM on Armbian (cooling-levels based on Temperature)
next.author: Amrut Prabhu
next.summary: Tune Armbian’s PWM fan curve using a Device Tree Overlay. This step-by-step guide shows how to locate the pwm-fan node, edit cooling-levels and temp-trips, compile a .dtbo overlay, enable it in armbianEnv.txt, and verify fan speed changes in Linux
